steps.title: Step 1 — Identify the task
steps.content: Quantitative completion. The hypothesis: holding other factors constant, as governments become MORE efficient, GDP-per-capita growth becomes MORE important to urban land expansion than population growth. The blank must use graph data to extend that proposal.

steps.title: Step 2 — Apply the hypothesis
steps.content: The graph shows that for Region 1, urban land expansion was driven LESS by GDP per capita (and more by population) in 2000–2014 than in 1970–2000. By the contrapositive of the proposal — if GDP's importance fell, efficiency must have fallen — this implies Region 1's national governments became LESS efficient in the later period.

steps.title: Step 3 — Per-choice elimination
steps.content:
• Choice B — 'slower rate of economic growth... despite increasing efficiency': introduces an economic-growth comparison the data don't address and contradicts the inferred direction of efficiency in Region 1. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Region 2 became more efficient... but several did not': focuses only on Region 2 and on within-region exceptions; doesn't reflect what the Region 1 data imply under the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — 'both regions generally became more efficient': contradicts the Region 1 inference that efficiency declined; overstates a uniform increase the graph doesn't support. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
